lastPrompt: is there a way to update the app so that i can see progress showing as its running?

message.content:
the last cycle drain i ran, took ages to complete, but it also consumed all of my quota. normally when i work on tasks manually, they take maximum 10 minutes and i can complete several dozen tasks before i reach my limits

debug/diagnose what went wrong and lets tighten up the observability and guard rails so that:

- i can better monitor what's happening when a run is running, including time elapsed, progress being made and tokens used
- there are guard rails around time and token usage -- when they breach the worker kills the session
- all of this should be logged into the log itself

the other thing i noticed is that some of my regular plugins (eg entire.io) didn't load up during this run, so something tells me that the way it was executed also played a broader role in broader side-effects than initially planned

content.text:
The previous progress feature is now part of a bigger ask: diagnose why the last drain consumed all quota and took ages, then add observability + hard guardrails (time AND tokens) that kill the worker on breach, all logged. Plus a side-investigation: why normal plugins (entire.io) didn't load — suggesting the execution method has wider side-effects than intended.

I'm in plan mode, so I'll investigate read-only and produce a plan. Let me start by mapping the relevant code and gathering runtime evidence in parallel.

input.description: Map orchestrator worker spawn
input.prompt:
Explore the repo at /Users/anton/src/drain-cycle. I need a precise, evidence-based map of how the orchestrator spawns and supervises the `claude -p` worker subprocess, for a diagnosis of runaway token/time usage.

Read `drain_cycle/orchestrator.py` in full and report, with exact file:line references and verbatim code snippets:

1. The exact subprocess invocation that spawns the worker: the full command list (`_CLAUDE_CMD` constant and how the prompt is appended), the `cwd`, whether `env=` is passed (and if so what), whether `stdout`/`stderr`/`stdin` are captured or inherited, `check=`, and `timeout=`.
2. The value of every timeout / limit constant (e.g. `_ISSUE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS`) and where each is used.
3. Exactly what happens on timeout (`subprocess.TimeoutExpired`): is the child process actually killed? Is the process *group* killed, or just the immediate child? (Look for `start_new_session`, `os.killpg`, `process.kill()`, `Popen` vs `run`.) This matters: `subprocess.run(timeout=...)` only kills the direct child, not grandchildren — note whether spawned grandchildren (e.g. MCP servers, sub-`claude` processes) would be orphaned.
4. The full loop structure: how issues are iterated, the halt paths (resolution-fail, setup-fail, timeout, not-Done), what `_revert_to_pre_halt_state` does, and whether the loop continues to the next issue after each outcome.
5. Whether there is ANY cap on tokens, turns, cost, or total wall-clock for the whole run (not just per-issue). Search for `--max-turns`, `--max-tokens`, token, cost, budget, anything.
6. Any retry logic around the worker spawn.

Be exhaustive and quote the actual code. Do not propose changes — just report what exists.

input.description: Map prompt and runlog
input.prompt:
Explore the repo at /Users/anton/src/drain-cycle. I'm diagnosing why an unattended drain run consumed an entire token quota and took a very long time per issue. The worker is a `claude -p --dangerously-skip-permissions` session given a prompt built by `drain_cycle/prompt.py`, and each completed issue is recorded by `drain_cycle/runlog.py`.

Read both files in full and report with exact file:line references and verbatim snippets:

A. From `drain_cycle/prompt.py` (`build(issue, worktree)` or similar):
   - The COMPLETE text of the prompt template the worker receives. Quote it verbatim.
   - Specifically identify any instructions that trigger heavy/expensive operations per issue: running slash-command skills (e.g. `/code-review-and-quality`), spawning sub-agents, multi-step review/fix/commit/push sequences, Linear MCP calls, test runs, etc. These multiply token usage.
   - Whether the prompt tells the worker to keep working until some condition, or anything that could cause long/looping sessions.

B. From `drain_cycle/runlog.py`:
   - The full RunLog schema: the dataclass fields, the per-entry dict shape (every key), and what `append_entry(...)` records.
   - Where the run-log files live (the `runs_dir()` path logic).
   - Whether ANY token usage, cost, turn count, or per-issue duration is currently recorded. Quote the exact fields.
   - How duration is computed if at all.

C. Note any other modules referenced by these two files that are relevant to logging or prompt construction.

Be exhaustive and quote actual code. Do not propose changes — just report what exists.

input.description: Investigate plugin/env loading
input.prompt:
Explore the repo at /Users/anton/src/drain-cycle. I'm investigating why, when the orchestrator spawns a `claude -p --dangerously-skip-permissions` worker, the user's normal Claude Code plugins (e.g. entire.io) did NOT load during the run — suggesting the spawned environment differs from a normal interactive `claude` session.

Search the whole repo (all of `drain_cycle/`, plus any config) and report with exact file:line references and verbatim snippets:

1. Everywhere the orchestrator or any module sets, mutates, strips, or passes environment variables to the worker subprocess. Search for: `env`, `os.environ`, `CLAUDE`, `CLAUDECODE`, `CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR`, `CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT`, `PATH`, `subprocess`, `Popen`, `run(`. Report whether the child inherits the full parent environment unchanged or a modified one.
2. The exact working directory (`cwd`) the worker runs in — it runs inside a git worktree. Read `drain_cycle/worktree.py` to find where worktrees are created (the `.worktrees/<identifier>` path under the target repo). Claude Code discovers project-level `.claude/` settings and `.mcp.json` relative to cwd — so note whether the worktree would contain the target repo's `.claude/` config (worktrees share the repo's tracked files) or not.
3. How the secrets/env loading works — read any `_load_secrets` or `.env` loading logic (likely in cli.py or a config/secrets module). Does it inject anything into the environment that the child inherits?
4. Whether `claude` is invoked with any flags that would disable plugins/MCP/settings (search for `--no-`, `--settings`, `--mcp-config`, `--strict-mcp-config`, `--append-system-prompt`, etc.) in the `_CLAUDE_CMD` constant and anywhere the command is assembled.
5. Read `docs/design-decisions.md` and report any decision entries about how the worker is invoked, env handling, `--dangerously-skip-permissions`, or plugin/MCP behaviour.

Be exhaustive and quote actual code. Do not propose changes — just report what exists.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have all the information I need. Let me create a comprehensive report with exact file:line references and verbatim snippets.

## Report: Token Consumption & Duration Analysis

Based on my complete read of `drain_cycle/prompt.py` and `drain_cycle/runlog.py`, here are the findings:

---

### A. PROMPT TEMPLATE AND EXPENSIVE OPERATIONS

**File:** `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/prompt.py`

**The complete prompt template** returned by `build(issue, worktree)` (lines 23–51):

```python
def build(issue: dict[str, Any], worktree: Path) -> str:
    title = issue.get("title", "")
    description = issue.get("description") or ""
    identifier = issue.get("identifier", "")

    preamble = (
        "---\n\n"
        "Execution instructions:\n"
        f"- Working directory: {worktree}\n"
        "- Base branch: main\n"
        f"- Completion sequence for issue {identifier} (run in this order, "
        "before marking Done):\n"
        "  1. Run `/code-review-and-quality` against the working-tree changes.\n"
        "  2. Fix any Critical or Required findings. Lower-severity findings "
        "are at your discretion.\n"
        "  3. Commit and push to main.\n"
        "  4. Post a short review-summary comment on the Linear issue via "
        "`mcp__claude_ai_Linear__save_comment` (count of findings by severity, "
        "fixed vs deferred).\n"
        "  5. Transition issue to Done via `mcp__claude_ai_Linear__save_issue` "
        '(state: "Done").\n'
    )

    return (
        f"# {title}\n\n"
        f"{description}\n\n"
        f"{preamble}\n"
        f"{_TAIL}\n"
    )
```

**The tail line** (lines 16–20):
```python
_TAIL = (
    "before marking Done: run /code-review-and-quality on the working-tree "
    "changes, fix Critical/Required findings, commit + push, then post a "
    "review-summary comment on the issue and transition to Done."
)
```

**Expensive operations per issue:**

1. **Lines 35 / preamble step 1 + tail line:** The worker is instructed to **run `/code-review-and-quality` skill** on working-tree changes. This is a multi-axis code review skill that:
   - Analyzes the full changeset
   - May require examining files, tests, and implementations
   - Uses an external agent (skill invocation = potential additional agent spawning or recursive Claude calls)
   - Runs **before** any commit, so every issue does this unconditionally

2. **Lines 36–37 / preamble step 2:** Fix Critical/Required findings — requires re-reading code, potentially re-implementing logic, and re-running `/code-review-and-quality` until passing.

3. **Lines 38–41 / preamble steps 3–5:** Four sequential operations all involving Linear MCP calls:
   - `git commit + push`
   - `mcp__claude_ai_Linear__save_comment` — save review summary
   - `mcp__claude_ai_Linear__save_issue` — transition to Done

**No loop/restart logic:** The prompt does not instruct the agent to retry or loop. It's a one-shot linear sequence. However, the `/code-review-and-quality` skill itself may involve multiple internal fix/re-check cycles, and if the agent doesn't complete all steps, the orchestrator will halt (not retry per issue).

---

### B. RUNLOG SCHEMA AND FIELDS

**File:** `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/runlog.py`

**RunLog dataclass** (lines 76–94):
```python
@dataclass
class RunLog:
    """Per-cycle run-log file. Construct once at orchestrator start."""

    cycle_id: str
    path: Path = field(init=False)
    entries: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list, init=False)
```

**Per-entry dict shape** (lines 104–126, `append_entry` method):
```python
def append_entry(
    self,
    *,
    issue_identifier: str,
    started_at: str,
    finished_at: str,
    exit_code: int,
    final_linear_state: str,
    worktree_path: str,
    halt_reason: str | None = None,
) -> None:
    self.entries.append(
        {
            "issue_identifier": issue_identifier,
            "started_at": started_at,
            "finished_at": finished_at,
            "exit_code": exit_code,
            "final_linear_state": final_linear_state,
            "worktree_path": worktree_path,
            "halt_reason": halt_reason,
        }
    )
```

**Per-entry required fields:**
- `issue_identifier` (str)
- `started_at` (ISO-8601 UTC string)
- `finished_at` (ISO-8601 UTC string)
- `exit_code` (int)
- `final_linear_state` (str, e.g. "Done", "Todo")
- `worktree_path` (str, absolute path)
- `halt_reason` (str | None; null on Done entries, populated on halt)

**Top-level JSON shape** (lines 129–134):
```python
payload = {
    "cycle_id": self.cycle_id,
    "cycle_duration_seconds": self._cycle_duration_seconds(),
    "entries": self.entries,
}
```

**Run-log directory** (lines 67–73):
```python
def runs_dir() -> Path:
    """Return the directory where per-cycle run logs are written."""
    return Path.home() / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"
```

Files are written to: `~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`  
Timestamp format: `%Y%m%dT%H%M%S%fZ` (UTC, microsecond resolution, line 100)

**Duration computation** (lines 136–141):
```python
def _cycle_duration_seconds(self) -> float:
    if not self.entries:
        return 0.0
    starts = [datetime.fromisoformat(e["started_at"]) for e in self.entries]
    finishes = [datetime.fromisoformat(e["finished_at"]) for e in self.entries]
    return (max(finishes) - min(starts)).total_seconds()
```

Duration is computed as `max(finished_at) - min(started_at)` across **all entries** — i.e., the wall-clock span from the first issue's start to the last issue's finish (the entire unattended session). Per-issue duration is not computed; individual durations must be derived from `finished_at - started_at` per entry.

**Token usage / cost tracking:** **NONE.** There is no field for tokens, cost, API usage, or any token-related metrics in the RunLog schema. The orchestrator and logging system are **completely opaque to token consumption.**

---

### C. REFERENCED MODULES

**Files that reference or use prompt/logging:**

1. **`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/orchestrator.py`** (lines 1–227):
   - **Line 143:** `agent_prompt = prompt.build(issue, worktree_path)` — builds the prompt for each issue
   - **Lines 146–151:** Spawns `claude -p --dangerously-skip-permissions` with the prompt
   - **Line 21:** `_CLAUDE_CMD = ["claude", "-p", "--dangerously-skip-permissions"]`
   - **Line 22–27:** Single-issue timeout: `_ISSUE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 3600.0` (1 hour hard cap per session)
   - **Lines 80, 102, 131, 167, 186, 214:** Calls to `log.append_entry(...)` to record outcomes
   - **Lines 154–176:** TimeoutExpired handling — halt after 3600s

2. **Linear module** (`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/linear.py`):
   - Called by orchestrator before spawning (line 119: `linear.set_state(issue["id"], _IN_PROGRESS_STATE_NAME)`)
   - Called after spawn (line 180–181: `refreshed = linear.get_issue(issue["id"])`)
   - The agent itself makes Linear MCP calls per the prompt, but those are **inside** the `claude -p` session, not orchestrator-visible

**Cost implications:**
- The only visibility into cost is the 3600-second timeout flag (line 22)
- No instrumentation of token usage in the orchestrator
- No per-issue token budget or logging
- `/code-review-and-quality` skill cost is entirely opaque — the orchestrator has no insight into whether it's a cheap lint check or a full multi-turn review agent

---

### DIAGNOSIS SUMMARY

The token quota exhaustion likely stems from:

1. **`/code-review-and-quality` is invoked per issue unconditionally** (preamble line 35, tail line 16–20). If this skill is a full-fledged multi-axis code review agent with its own sub-agents or extended reasoning, it multiplies token usage significantly.

2. **Fix cycles:** Step 2 of the preamble says "Fix any Critical or Required findings" — if the first review finds many issues, fixing + re-reviewing creates multiple passes through the skill, each expensive.

3. **No token budget or per-issue cap:** The orchestrator has no mechanism to track or limit token use per issue. The only hard cap is 3600 seconds (1 hour wall-clock time), which doesn't throttle token burn if the session is doing heavy compute.

4. **No logging of token/cost metrics:** The run-log records only wall-clock timing (`started_at`, `finished_at`). Without recording tokens consumed, exit token count, or cost per issue, there's no post-hoc observability into which issue(s) consumed the quota. The duration column (`finished_at - started_at`) is wall-clock time, not token time.

5. **Linear MCP calls inside spawned session:** The agent makes MCP calls to save comments and transition state (preamble steps 4–5). If the worker is running inside a `claude -p` session that itself is instrumented to log to an external service, those MCP calls might be re-invoking the Linear server in verbose/instrumented mode, multiplying network/parsing overhead.

---

### EXACT FILE:LINE CITATIONS

| Finding | File | Lines | Verbatim Text |
|---------|------|-------|----------------|
| Prompt template | `prompt.py` | 46–51 | `return (f"# {title}\n\n{description}\n\n{preamble}\n{_TAIL}\n")` |
| Code review instruction (preamble) | `prompt.py` | 35 | `"  1. Run \`/code-review-and-quality\` against the working-tree changes.\n"` |
| Code review instruction (tail) | `prompt.py` | 16–20 | `_TAIL = ("before marking Done: run /code-review-and-quality on the working-tree changes, fix Critical/Required findings, commit + push, then post a review-summary comment on the issue and transition to Done.")` |
| Fix loop instruction | `prompt.py` | 36–37 | `"  2. Fix any Critical or Required findings. Lower-severity findings are at your discretion.\n"` |
| Linear MCP calls | `prompt.py` | 39–43 | Steps 4–5: save_comment + save_issue with state: Done |
| RunLog fields | `runlog.py` | 104–126 | All seven fields in `append_entry` signature |
| Top-level JSON | `runlog.py` | 129–134 | `cycle_id`, `cycle_duration_seconds`, `entries` |
| Runs directory | `runlog.py` | 67–73 | `return Path.home() / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"` |
| Duration formula | `runlog.py` | 136–141 | `(max(finishes) - min(starts)).total_seconds()` |
| Timeout per issue | `orchestrator.py` | 22–27 | `_ISSUE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 3600.0` |
| Prompt building | `orchestrator.py` | 143 | `agent_prompt = prompt.build(issue, worktree_path)` |
| No token tracking | `runlog.py` | Entire schema | No token/cost/API-usage field anywhere |

Now let me generate a comprehensive report based on all the evidence I've gathered:

def _revert_to_pre_halt_state(

## Investigation Report: Environment Variables and Configuration in Spawned Worker

Based on my exhaustive search of the `drain-cycle` repository, here are my findings regarding how the orchestrator spawns the worker and why plugins may not load:

---

### 1. **Subprocess Environment Variables — Full Parent Environment Inherited Unchanged**

**File:** `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/orchestrator.py`, lines 146–151

result = subprocess.run(
    [*_CLAUDE_CMD, agent_prompt],
    cwd=worktree_path,
    check=False,
    timeout=_ISSUE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS,
)
```

**Finding:** The `subprocess.run()` call does **NOT** pass an `env=` parameter. This means the child process **inherits the full parent environment unchanged**. Python's `subprocess.run()` defaults to inheriting the parent's `os.environ` when no `env` argument is provided.

No environment variables are stripped, modified, or explicitly set anywhere in the orchestrator or any calling module before this invocation.

---

### 2. **The `_CLAUDE_CMD` Constant — No Env-Modifying Flags**

**File:** `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/orchestrator.py`, line 21

```python
_CLAUDE_CMD = ["claude", "-p", "--dangerously-skip-permissions"]
```

**Finding:** The command is hardcoded to:
- `claude` — invokes the `claude` binary on PATH
- `-p` — prompt mode (single-turn, non-interactive)
- `--dangerously-skip-permissions` — skips permission checks

There are **no flags that disable plugins, MCP, settings, or configuration loading**. Notably, absent are:
- `--no-plugins`, `--no-mcp`, `--no-settings`, `--strict-mcp-config`
- `--settings` / `--mcp-config` (which would override config location)
- Any `--append-system-prompt` or system prompt overrides

---

### 3. **Working Directory — Inside a Git Worktree (Inherits Tracked `.claude/` Files)**

**File:** `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/worktree.py`, lines 21–31

```python
def add(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> Path:
    """Create a worktree branched off ``main`` for ``identifier``.

    Returns the absolute path to the new worktree.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    _run_git(
        ["worktree", "add", "-b", identifier, str(worktree_path), BASE_BRANCH],
        cwd=repo,
    )
    return worktree_path
```

Where `WORKTREE_DIR = ".worktrees"` (line 18).

**Finding:** Each issue runs in `.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` — a `git worktree` created by `git worktree add -b <identifier> <path> main`. This worktree:
- **Is a sparse working tree sharing tracked files from the target repo**
- **WILL contain the target repo's `.claude/` directory** (tracked by git as part of the repo)
- **WILL contain the target repo's `.entire/` directory** if tracked

The orchestrator spawns the worker with `cwd=worktree_path`, so Claude Code will discover:
- `.claude/settings.json` and `.claude/settings.local.json` at the worktree root
- `.claude/agents/` if any exist
- `.entire/settings.json` and hooks if the entire.io integration is tracked

**For the drain-cycle repo specifically:** The worktree inherits these tracked files:
- `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/.claude/settings.json` — contains hooks for entire.io
- `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/.claude/settings.local.json` — contains MCP/Linear permissions
- `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/.entire/settings.json` — entire CLI settings

---

### 4. **Secrets and Environment Loading — Happens in Parent Only**

**File:** `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/cli.py`, lines 31–41

```python
def _load_secrets() -> None:
    """Populate ``os.environ`` from the first ``.env`` that defines each key.

    ``~/.drain-cycle/.env`` sits beside ``repos.yml`` and the run logs,
    so an installed tool finds its secret there. The repo-root ``.env``
    is a dev-checkout fallback only — once installed as a uv tool the
    package lives in an isolated env where that path has no ``.env``.
    ``$HOME`` is resolved per call so tests can redirect it.
    """
    load_dotenv(Path.home() / ".drain-cycle" / ".env")
    load_dotenv(_REPO_ENV)
```

Called from `cli.main()` (line 52) before orchestrator invocation.

**Finding:**
- **Secrets are loaded into the parent process** (`drain-cycle` CLI) via `load_dotenv()` with `override=False` (defaults to this behavior)
- **Load order:** shell-exported → `~/.drain-cycle/.env` → repo-root `.env`, first hit wins
- **The child process inherits the parent's `os.environ`**, so `LINEAR_API_KEY` and any other shell-exported variables are passed through

No `.env` file is explicitly loaded or injected into the child's environment; it's all in the parent's inherited `os.environ`.

---

### 5. **Linear API Key Access — Read by Child from Inherited Environment**

**File:** `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/linear.py`, lines 25–28

```python
def _post(query: str, variables: dict[str, Any] | None = None) -> dict[str, Any]:
    key = os.environ.get("LINEAR_API_KEY")
    if not key:
        raise RuntimeError("LINEAR_API_KEY is not set")
```

**Finding:** The child process can access `LINEAR_API_KEY` via the inherited environment.

---

### 6. **Design Decisions — No Documented Env Handling or Plugin Behavior**

**File:** `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/docs/design-decisions.md`

Searched the entire document for mentions of:
- Environment variables
- Plugin/MCP loading
- Settings discovery
- `--dangerously-skip-permissions` behavior

**Findings:**

**Decision 2** (lines 15–22) covers `--dangerously-skip-permissions` but focuses on blast radius, not how plugins are affected:

> Every spawned session runs with `--dangerously-skip-permissions`. The agent can run any tool on any path inside its worktree, including shell commands, file writes, and network calls, with no operator approval.
>
> **Why accepted.** The single-operator personal-product context: target repos are mine, the Linear workspace is mine, the machine is mine. The point of the tool is removing prompts; gating them defeats the purpose.

**Decision 6** (lines 59–71) covers `.env` handling but only for the parent process:

> Secrets load before any module reads `os.environ`, first hit wins: shell-exported vars → `~/.drain-cycle/.env` → the drain-cycle repo root `.env` (dev-checkout fallback).

No decision entry explicitly addresses:
- How the child process discovers `.claude/` config
- Whether plugins/MCP load in the spawned session
- Any known limitations of `--dangerously-skip-permissions` affecting plugin discovery

---

### 7. **Summary — Why Plugins May Not Load in the Spawned Worker**

**The Code Itself Is Not Responsible:**

- The orchestrator passes the **full parent environment** to the child
- The child runs in a **worktree that inherits tracked `.claude/` config**
- No environment variables are stripped, modified, or disabled

**Potential Root Causes (Speculation, Not in Code):**

1. **`claude -p` prompt-mode behavior:** The `-p` flag may have different plugin/config discovery or initialization logic than interactive mode
2. **`--dangerously-skip-permissions` interaction:** This flag may bypass hooks or MCP initialization (not documented in design decisions)
3. **Claude Code version/feature differences:** The child process may be running a different version or build of Claude Code
4. **PATH differences:** Plugins like `entire.io` may not be discoverable if the child's `$PATH` differs (though code doesn't modify PATH)
5. **stdin/stdout/stderr handling:** Prompt mode may not initialize interactive features that plugins depend on

**What the Code Shows:**

- No explicit env-stripping occurs
- No config-location overrides occur
- The full parent environment is inherited
- The `.claude/` directory is present in the worktree
- `--dangerously-skip-permissions` is the only special flag

---

### Critical Code References

| Aspect | File | Lines | Code |
|--------|------|-------|------|
| Subprocess invocation | `orchestrator.py` | 146–151 | `subprocess.run([*_CLAUDE_CMD, agent_prompt], cwd=worktree_path, ...)` |
| Command definition | `orchestrator.py` | 21 | `_CLAUDE_CMD = ["claude", "-p", "--dangerously-skip-permissions"]` |
| Working directory path | `worktree.py` | 18, 26 | `.worktrees/<identifier>` created via `git worktree add` |
| Secrets loading | `cli.py` | 31–41 | `load_dotenv()` into parent's `os.environ` |
| Linear auth | `linear.py` | 26 | `key = os.environ.get("LINEAR_API_KEY")` in child |
| No env manipulation | `orchestrator.py` | 146–151 | **No `env=` parameter** → defaults to full parent inheritance |

---

**Conclusion:** The orchestrator code does not manipulate environment variables or disable plugins. The issue lies either in Claude Code's `-p` prompt-mode behavior, `--dangerously-skip-permissions` side effects, or runtime differences between the spawned and interactive sessions — none of which are visible in or documented by the drain-cycle repository code.

)

**Finding:** The `subprocess.run()` call does **NOT** pass an `env=` parameter. This means the child process **inherits the full parent environment unchanged**. Python's `subprocess.run()` defaults to inheriting the parent's `os.environ` when no `env` argument is provided.

No environment variables are stripped, modified, or explicitly set anywhere in the orchestrator or any calling module before this invocation.

```

- `claude` — invokes the `claude` binary on PATH
- `-p` — prompt mode (single-turn, non-interactive)
- `--dangerously-skip-permissions` — skips permission checks

There are **no flags that disable plugins, MCP, settings, or configuration loading**. Notably, absent are:
- `--no-plugins`, `--no-mcp`, `--no-settings`, `--strict-mcp-config`
- `--settings` / `--mcp-config` (which would override config location)
- Any `--append-system-prompt` or system prompt overrides

---

### 3. **Working Directory — Inside a Git Worktree (Inherits Tracked `.claude/` Files)**

**File:** `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/worktree.py`, lines 21–31

```python
def add(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> Path:
    """Create a worktree branched off ``main`` for ``identifier``.

    Returns the absolute path to the new worktree.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    _run_git(
        ["worktree", "add", "-b", identifier, str(worktree_path), BASE_BRANCH],
        cwd=repo,
    )
    return worktree_path
```

Where `WORKTREE_DIR = ".worktrees"` (line 18).

**Finding:** Each issue runs in `.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/` — a `git worktree` created by `git worktree add -b <identifier> <path> main`. This worktree:
- **Is a sparse working tree sharing tracked files from the target repo**
- **WILL contain the target repo's `.claude/` directory** (tracked by git as part of the repo)
- **WILL contain the target repo's `.entire/` directory** if tracked

The orchestrator spawns the worker with `cwd=worktree_path`, so Claude Code will discover:
- `.claude/settings.json` and `.claude/settings.local.json` at the worktree root
- `.claude/agents/` if any exist
- `.entire/settings.json` and hooks if the entire.io integration is tracked

**For the drain-cycle repo specifically:** The worktree inherits these tracked files:
- `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/.claude/settings.json` — contains hooks for entire.io
- `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/.claude/settings.local.json` — contains MCP/Linear permissions
- `/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/.entire/settings.json` — entire CLI settings

2.1.150 (Claude Code)
---HELP---
Usage: claude [options] [command] [prompt]

Claude Code - starts an interactive session by default, use -p/--print for
non-interactive output

Arguments:
  prompt                                            Your prompt

Options:
  --add-dir <directories...>                        Additional directories to allow tool access to
  --agent <agent>                                   Agent for the current session. Overrides the 'agent' setting.
  --agents <json>                                   JSON object defining custom agents (e.g. '{"reviewer": {"description": "Reviews code", "prompt": "You are a code reviewer"}}')
  --allow-dangerously-skip-permissions              Enable bypassing all permission checks as an option, without it being enabled by default. Recommended only for sandboxes with no internet access.
  --allowedTools, --allowed-tools <tools...>        Comma or space-separated list of tool names to allow (e.g. "Bash(git *) Edit")
  --append-system-prompt <prompt>                   Append a system prompt to the default system prompt
  --bare                                            Minimal mode: skip hooks, LSP, plugin sync, attribution, auto-memory, background prefetches, keychain reads, and CLAUDE.md auto-discovery. Sets CLAUDE_CODE_SIMPLE=1. Anthropic auth is strictly ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or apiKeyHelper via --settings (OAuth and keychain are never read). 3P providers (Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry) use their own credentials. Skills still resolve via /skill-name. Explicitly provide context via: --system-prompt[-file], --append-system-prompt[-file], --add-dir (CLAUDE.md dirs), --mcp-config, --settings, --agents, --plugin-dir.
  --betas <betas...>                                Beta headers to include in API requests (API key users only)
  --brief                                           Enable SendUserMessage tool for agent-to-user communication
  --chrome                                          Enable Claude in Chrome integration
  -c, --continue                                    Continue the most recent conversation in the current directory
  --dangerously-skip-permissions                    Bypass all permission checks. Recommended only for sandboxes with no internet access.
  -d, --debug [filter]                              Enable debug mode with optional category filtering (e.g., "api,hooks" or "!1p,!file")
  --debug-file <path>                               Write debug logs to a specific file path (implicitly enables debug mode)
  --disable-slash-commands                          Disable all skills
  --disallowedTools, --disallowed-tools <tools...>  Comma or space-separated list of tool names to deny (e.g. "Bash(git *) Edit")
  --effort <level>                                  Effort level for the current session (low, medium, high, xhigh, max)
  --exclude-dynamic-system-prompt-sections          Move per-machine sections (cwd, env info, memory paths, git status) from the system prompt into the first user message. Improves cross-user prompt-cache reuse. Only applies with the default system prompt (ignored with --system-prompt). (default: false)
  --fallback-model <model>                          Enable automatic fallback to specified model when default model is overloaded (only works with --print)
  --file <specs...>                                 File resources to download at startup. Format: file_id:relative_path (e.g., --file file_abc:doc.txt file_def:img.png)
  --fork-session                                    When resuming, create a new session ID instead of reusing the original (use with --resume or --continue)
  --from-pr [value]                                 Resume a session linked to a PR by PR number/URL, or open interactive picker with optional search term
  -h, --help                                        Display help for command
  --ide                                             Automatically connect to IDE on startup if exactly one valid IDE is available
  --include-hook-events                             Include all hook lifecycle events in the output stream (only works with --output-format=stream-json)
  --include-partial-messages                        Include partial message chunks as they arrive (only works with --print and --output-format=stream-json)
  --input-format <format>                           Input format (only works with --print): "text" (default), or "stream-json" (realtime streaming input) (choices: "text", "stream-json")
  --json-schema <schema>                            JSON Schema for structured output validation. Example: {"type":"object","properties":{"name":{"type":"string"}},"required":["name"]}
  --max-budget-usd <amount>                         Maximum dollar amount to spend on API calls (only works with --print)
  --mcp-config <configs...>                         Load MCP servers from JSON files or strings (space-separated)
  --mcp-debug                                       [DEPRECATED. Use --debug instead] Enable MCP debug mode (shows MCP server errors)
  --model <model>                                   Model for the current session. Provide an alias for the latest model (e.g. 'sonnet' or 'opus') or a model's full name (e.g. 'claude-sonnet-4-6').
  -n, --name <name>                                 Set a display name for this session (shown in the prompt box, /resume picker, and terminal title)
  --no-chrome                                       Disable Claude in Chrome integration
  --no-session-persistence                          Disable session persistence - sessions will not be saved to disk and cannot be resumed (only works with --print)
  --output-format <format>                          Output format (only works with --print): "text" (default), "json" (single result), or "stream-json" (realtime streaming) (choices: "text", "json", "stream-json")
  --permission-mode <mode>                          Permission mode to use for the session (choices: "acceptEdits", "auto", "bypassPermissions", "default", "dontAsk", "plan")
  --plugin-dir <path>                               Load a plugin from a directory or .zip for this session only (repeatable: --plugin-dir A --plugin-dir B.zip) (default: [])
  --plugin-url <url>                                Fetch a plugin .zip from a URL for this session only (repeatable: --plugin-url A --plugin-url B) (default: [])
  -p, --print                                       Print response and exit (useful for pipes). Note: The workspace trust dialog is skipped when Claude is run in non-interactive mode (via -p, or when stdout is not a TTY, e.g. piped or redirected output). Only use this in directories you trust. Settings files that fail validation are silently ignored in this mode (no error dialog is shown).
  --remote-control [name]                           Start an interactive session with Remote Control enabled (optionally named)
  --remote-control-session-name-prefix <prefix>     Prefix for auto-generated Remote Control session names (default: hostname)
  --replay-user-messages                            Re-emit user messages from stdin back on stdout for acknowledgment (only works with --input-format=stream-json and --output-format=stream-json)
  -r, --resume [value]                              Resume a conversation by session ID, or open interactive picker with optional search term
  --session-id <uuid>                               Use a specific session ID for the conversation (must be a valid UUID)
  --setting-sources <sources>                       Comma-separated list of setting sources to load (user, project, local).
  --settings <file-or-json>                         Path to a settings JSON file or a JSON string to load additional settings from
  --strict-mcp-config                               Only use MCP servers from --mcp-config, ignoring all other MCP configurations
  --system-prompt <prompt>                          System prompt to use for the session
  --tmux                                            Create a tmux session for the worktree (requires --worktree). Uses iTerm2 native panes when available; use --tmux=classic for traditional tmux.
  --tools <tools...>                                Specify the list of available tools from the built-in set. Use "" to disable all tools, "default" to use all tools, or specify tool names (e.g. "Bash,Edit,Read").
  --verbose                                         Override verbose mode setting from config
  -v, --version                                     Output the version number
  -w, --worktree [name]                             Create a new git worktree for this session (optionally specify a name)

Commands:
  agents [options]                                  Manage background agents
  auth                                              Manage authentication
  auto-mode                                         Inspect auto mode classifier configuration
  doctor                                            Check the health of your Claude Code auto-updater. Note: The workspace trust dialog is skipped and stdio servers from .mcp.json are spawned for health checks. Only use this command in directories you trust.
  install [options] [target]                        Install Claude Code native build. Use [target] to specify version (stable, latest, or specific version)
  mcp                                               Configure and manage MCP servers
  plugin|plugins                                    Manage Claude Code plugins
  project                                           Manage Claude Code project state
  setup-token                                       Set up a long-lived authentication token (requires Claude subscription)
  ultrareview [options] [target]                    Run a cloud-hosted multi-agent code review of the current branch (or a PR number / base branch) and print the findings
  update|upgrade                                    Check for updates and install if available

content.text: Now I have hard evidence. The burning run (`...073843985001Z.json`) processed 5 issues in 76.7 min: ABA-204 **18m**, ABA-205 **23m**, ABA-134 **21m**, ABA-188 **3m**, ABA-133 **11m** (halted). Manual work is ~10 min/task — these ran 2× longer *and* each runs a full `/code-review-and-quality` multi-agent review. And `entire` is **not** in `enabledPlugins` — it's a separate integration. Let me confirm the actual token burn from the session transcripts and check the model + entire setup.

input.command:
cd ~/.claude/projects 2>/dev/null || exit 0
echo "=== project session dirs for the worktrees (pde-skills/stock-review/agent-skills ABA-*) ==="
ls -d *pde-skills*ABA-204* *pde-skills*ABA-205* *stock-review*ABA-134* *agent-skills*ABA-188* *stock-review*ABA-133* 2>/dev/null
echo
echo "=== token usage per worktree session (sum input+output+cache from JSONL usage) ==="
for d in $(ls -d *worktrees*ABA-204* *worktrees*ABA-205* *worktrees*ABA-134* *worktrees*ABA-188* *worktrees*ABA-133* 2>/dev/null); do
  f=$(ls -t "$d"/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null | head -1)
  [ -z "$f" ] && continue
  echo "--- $d"
  python3 - "$f" <<'PY'
import json,sys
f=sys.argv[1]
it=ot=ci=cr=msgs=0
model=set()
for line in open(f):
    try: o=json.loads(line)
    except: continue
    u=(o.get("message") or {}).get("usage") or o.get("usage")
    m=(o.get("message") or {}).get("model")
    if m: model.add(m)
    if u:
        msgs+=1
        it+=u.get("input_tokens",0); ot+=u.get("output_tokens",0)
        ci+=u.get("cache_creation_input_tokens",0); cr+=u.get("cache_read_input_tokens",0)
print(f"  models={sorted(model)}")
print(f"  assistant_msgs={msgs}  input={it:,}  output={ot:,}  cache_create={ci:,}  cache_read={cr:,}")
print(f"  TOTAL billed-ish input(in+cc+cr)={it+ci+cr:,}  output={ot:,}")
PY
done

input.command:
P=/Users/anton/.claude/projects
echo "=== matching worktree session dirs ==="
ls -d "$P"/*worktrees*ABA-204* "$P"/*worktrees*ABA-205* "$P"/*worktrees*ABA-134* "$P"/*worktrees*ABA-188* "$P"/*worktrees*ABA-133* 2>/dev/null
echo
for d in "$P"/*worktrees*ABA-204* "$P"/*worktrees*ABA-205* "$P"/*worktrees*ABA-134* "$P"/*worktrees*ABA-188* "$P"/*worktrees*ABA-133*; do
  [ -d "$d" ] || continue
  f=$(ls -t "$d"/*.jsonl 2>/dev/null | head -1)
  [ -z "$f" ] && continue
  python3 - "$d" "$f" <<'PY'
import json,sys,os
d,f=sys.argv[1],sys.argv[2]
it=ot=ci=cr=msgs=0; model=set()
for line in open(f):
    try: o=json.loads(line)

PY
done

content.text:
The token burn is now quantified and damning. Across the 5 issues, all on **Opus** (`claude-opus-4-7`):

| Issue | Turns | Cache-read tokens | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| ABA-204 | 113 | 16.5M | 270K |
| ABA-205 | 234 | **43.1M** | 273K |
| ABA-134 | 192 | 34.1M | 341K |
| ABA-188 | 49 | 2.7M | 36K |
| ABA-133 | 98 | 11.3M | 141K |
| **Total** | **686** | **~107.7M** | **~1.06M** |

**~108M tokens of cache-read on Opus in 77 minutes** — that's the quota. The driver is **turn count** (234 turns on one issue): every turn re-reads the full accumulated context, and each session reloads the entire global CLAUDE.md rule-import chain fresh. The `/code-review-and-quality` skill + fix loops inflate turns. And `entire` is a standalone binary not registered as a user hook/plugin — so headless `-p` never invoked its checkpointing.

Let me confirm the exact orchestrator/runlog structure before designing.

file.content:
"""Drain a cycle by iterating over its sorted Todo/Backlog issues.

Halt-on-not-Done, the orchestrator-owned Todo→In-Progress transition, the
run-log artefact, and the inspectable-halt UX all live here. The halt-message
helper ``_halt_message`` is the single source of truth for the operator-facing
halt string — emitted both on stderr and into the run-log entry's
``halt_reason`` field.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import subprocess
import sys
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path

from . import linear, prompt, runlog, worktree
from .repos import RepoResolutionError, Repos

_DONE_STATE_TYPE = "completed"
_IN_PROGRESS_STATE_NAME = "In Progress"
_CLAUDE_CMD = ["claude", "-p", "--dangerously-skip-permissions"]
_ISSUE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 3600.0
"""Outer cap on one spawned ``claude -p`` session. A session that exceeds
this converts to a halt — same exit-1 + run-log entry + Halt: stderr line
as any other halt — so a hung agent advances the cycle to operator
attention instead of stalling indefinitely. Sized for one long unattended
issue; raise locally if a single issue legitimately takes longer."""
_UNRESOLVED_WORKTREE_DISPLAY = "<unresolved>"
"""Worktree-path placeholder for the pre-spawn resolution-halt path.
No path has been chosen yet — the issue couldn't be mapped to a target
repo — so the run-log entry and stderr halt line carry this marker
rather than a misleading fake path."""


def _now_iso() -> str:
    return datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()


def _halt_message(identifier: str, state_name: str, worktree_path: Path) -> str:
    """Single source of truth for the halt UX.

    The same string lands on stderr (the operator's grep anchor) and in
    the run-log entry's ``halt_reason`` field — so kill-condition tooling
    reads the same human-readable explanation the operator saw at halt
    time.
    """
    return f"Halt: {identifier} (final state: {state_name}) at {worktree_path}"


def _revert_to_pre_halt_state(
    issue_id: str, *, target_state_name: str, pre_revert_state_name: str
) -> tuple[str, str | None]:
    """Restore Linear state on halt; return ``(state_to_report, error_msg)``.

    The orchestrator transitions issues Todo→In Progress before spawning the
    agent. When the run halts, that In-Progress flag leaves the issue outside
    ``_PENDING_STATE_TYPES`` so a re-run silently skips it. This helper
    reverses the transition and re-fetches to confirm.

    On revert success, returns the refreshed state name and ``None``.
    On revert failure, returns ``pre_revert_state_name`` (the state the
    issue is actually still in, so the operator can find it) plus the
    exception message — non-fatal. A failed refresh after a
    successful revert falls back to ``target_state_name``, since we trust
    the mutation landed even if the read-back didn't.
    """
    try:
        linear.set_state(issue_id, target_state_name)
    except Exception as exc:
        return pre_revert_state_name, str(exc)
    try:
        refreshed = linear.get_issue(issue_id)
    except Exception:
        return target_state_name, None
    return refreshed["state"]["name"], None


def run(repos: Repos) -> int:
    cycle_id = linear.current_cycle_id()
    log = runlog.RunLog(cycle_id=cycle_id)
    issues = linear.pending_issues(cycle_id)
    if not issues:
        print(f"Cycle {cycle_id} has no Todo/Backlog issues — nothing to do.")
        return 0

    for issue in issues:
        identifier = issue["identifier"]
        print(f"drain-cycle: picked {identifier}: {issue['title']}", file=sys.stderr)

        started_at = _now_iso()
        try:
            target_repo = repos.resolve(issue)
        except RepoResolutionError as exc:
            # Pre-spawn resolution halt: no Linear state was moved, so no
            # revert is attempted. The worktree path is the ``<unresolved>``
            # placeholder since no repo was chosen.
            state_name = issue["state"]["name"]
            halt_reason = (
                f"{_halt_message(identifier, state_name, Path(_UNRESOLVED_WORKTREE_DISPLAY))}"
                f" — {exc}"
            )
            log.append_entry(
                issue_identifier=identifier,
                started_at=started_at,
                finished_at=_now_iso(),
                exit_code=-1,
                final_linear_state=state_name,
                REDACTED,
                halt_reason=halt_reason,
            )
            print(halt_reason, file=sys.stderr)
            return 1

        try:
            worktree_path = worktree.add(target_repo, identifier)
            # Orchestrator owns the Todo→In Progress half so the lifecycle
            # doesn't depend on the spawned agent's compliance. The agent
            # still owns the …→Done half via Linear MCP — see prompt.py tail.
            linear.set_state(issue["id"], _IN_PROGRESS_STATE_NAME)
        except Exception as exc:
            # Convert any pre-spawn failure into a recorded halt rather than
            # a traceback: write a run-log entry with the planned worktree
            # path, print the halt message, exit non-zero. Subsequent issues
            # are not attempted — same contract as a spawn-time halt.
            planned_path = target_repo / worktree.WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
            state_name = issue["state"]["name"]
            halt_reason = (
                f"{_halt_message(identifier, state_name, planned_path)}"
                f" — setup failed: {exc}"
            )
            log.append_entry(
                issue_identifier=identifier,
                started_at=started_at,
                finished_at=_now_iso(),
                exit_code=-1,
                final_linear_state=state_name,
                worktree_path=str(planned_path),
                halt_reason=halt_reason,
            )
            print(halt_reason, file=sys.stderr)
            return 1

        agent_prompt = prompt.build(issue, worktree_path)

        try:
            result = subprocess.run(
                [*_CLAUDE_CMD, agent_prompt],
                cwd=worktree_path,
                check=False,
                timeout=_ISSUE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS,
            )
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            original_state_name = issue["state"]["name"]
            effective_state, revert_error = _revert_to_pre_halt_state(
                issue["id"],
                target_state_name=original_state_name,
                pre_revert_state_name=_IN_PROGRESS_STATE_NAME,
            )
            halt_reason = (
                f"{_halt_message(identifier, effective_state, worktree_path)}"
                f" — claude -p exceeded {_ISSUE_TIMEOUT_SECONDS:.0f}s timeout"
            )
            if revert_error is not None:
                halt_reason += (
                    f"; revert to {original_state_name!r} failed: {revert_error}"
                )
            log.append_entry(
                issue_identifier=identifier,
                started_at=started_at,
                finished_at=_now_iso(),
                exit_code=-1,
                final_linear_state=effective_state,
                worktree_path=str(worktree_path),
                halt_reason=halt_reason,
            )
            print(halt_reason, file=sys.stderr)
            return 1
        finished_at = _now_iso()

        refreshed = linear.get_issue(issue["id"])
        post_spawn_state = refreshed["state"]["name"]
        is_done = refreshed["state"]["type"] == _DONE_STATE_TYPE

        if is_done:
            # Append unconditionally for every attempted issue.
            log.append_entry(
                issue_identifier=identifier,
                started_at=started_at,
                finished_at=finished_at,
                exit_code=result.returncode,
                final_linear_state=post_spawn_state,
                worktree_path=str(worktree_path),
                halt_reason=None,
            )
            worktree.remove(target_repo, worktree_path)
            print(f"drain-cycle: {identifier} done; worktree removed.", file=sys.stderr)
            continue

        # Not-Done halt: revert to the pre-halt state so a re-run picks
        # this issue back up instead of silently skipping it.
        original_state_name = issue["state"]["name"]
        effective_state, revert_error = _revert_to_pre_halt_state(
            issue["id"],
            target_state_name=original_state_name,
            pre_revert_state_name=post_spawn_state,
        )
        halt_reason = _halt_message(identifier, effective_state, worktree_path)
        if revert_error is not None:
            halt_reason += (
                f" — revert to {original_state_name!r} failed: {revert_error}"
            )
        # halt_reason carries the same string also printed to stderr below
        # so the on-disk and terminal surfaces cannot drift.
        log.append_entry(
            issue_identifier=identifier,
            started_at=started_at,
            finished_at=finished_at,
            exit_code=result.returncode,
            final_linear_state=effective_state,
            worktree_path=str(worktree_path),
            halt_reason=halt_reason,
        )
        print(halt_reason, file=sys.stderr)
        return 1

    return 0

content.content:
1	"""Per-cycle run-log artefact.
2	
3	Each ``drain-cycle`` invocation produces a single JSON file at
4	``~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`` capturing one
5	entry per attempted issue. The filename embeds the run-start timestamp
6	(UTC, ``%Y%m%dT%H%M%S%fZ``) so re-running ``drain-cycle`` against the
7	same cycle writes a new file instead of clobbering the prior one.
8	Downstream consumers glob the directory and group by ``cycle_id``
9	(carried inside each file).
10	
11	* KR1 (completion %) is computed from ``entries[].final_linear_state``
12	  merged across every file sharing the same ``cycle_id``.
13	* Cycle-drain self-grading reads every file across cycles.
14	* The kill condition (KR1 < 50%) merges per-cycle then thresholds.
15	
16	Schema:
17	
18	::
19	
20	    {
21	      "cycle_id":               "<linear-cycle-uuid>",
22	      "cycle_duration_seconds": <float>,
23	      "entries":                [
24	        {
25	          "issue_identifier":   "ABA-NNN",

file.content:
"""Per-cycle run-log artefact.

Each ``drain-cycle`` invocation produces a single JSON file at
``~/.drain-cycle/runs/<cycle-id>-<run-timestamp>.json`` capturing one
entry per attempted issue. The filename embeds the run-start timestamp
(UTC, ``%Y%m%dT%H%M%S%fZ``) so re-running ``drain-cycle`` against the
same cycle writes a new file instead of clobbering the prior one.
Downstream consumers glob the directory and group by ``cycle_id``
(carried inside each file).

* KR1 (completion %) is computed from ``entries[].final_linear_state``
  merged across every file sharing the same ``cycle_id``.
* Cycle-drain self-grading reads every file across cycles.
* The kill condition (KR1 < 50%) merges per-cycle then thresholds.

Schema:

::

    {
      "cycle_id":               "<linear-cycle-uuid>",
      "cycle_duration_seconds": <float>,
      "entries":                [
        {
          "issue_identifier":   "ABA-NNN",
          "started_at":         "<iso-8601 UTC>",
          "finished_at":        "<iso-8601 UTC>",
          "exit_code":          <int>,
          "final_linear_state": "Done" | "Todo" | ...,
          "worktree_path":      "<absolute path>",
          "halt_reason":        null | "<orchestrator stderr halt line>",
        },
        ...
      ],
    }

``halt_reason`` is ``null`` on every Done entry (the agent flipped state
as required). On the orchestrator's halt entry it is the exact string
also written to stderr — both surfaces are produced by the same
``_halt_message`` helper in ``orchestrator.py`` so the on-disk and
terminal values cannot drift. Non-last entries are
``null`` by construction: the orchestrator returns on first halt, so
anything before the halt is a Done entry.

``cycle_duration_seconds`` is computed automatically on every persist as
``max(finished_at) - min(started_at)`` across ``entries`` (``0.0`` when
empty). This is the KR2 grading proxy — "how long was the agent doing
execution for me unattended" stands in for "operator hands-off time" —
and replaces the originally specified ``time_spent`` block, which
required manual self-report at cycle close and was never going to be
filled in.

Persistence is incremental: ``append_entry`` re-serialises the whole dict
on every call, so a mid-run crash still leaves a well-formed file with
every issue attempted so far. Cycle scale (≤ 15 issues) makes the cost
irrelevant.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any


def runs_dir() -> Path:
    """Return the directory where per-cycle run logs are written.

    Resolved on every call (not module-import time) so tests can redirect
    via ``monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", ...)`` after the module is imported.
    """
    return Path.home() / ".drain-cycle" / "runs"


@dataclass
class RunLog:
    """Per-cycle run-log file. Construct once at orchestrator start.

    Writing the initial ``{cycle_id, cycle_duration_seconds: 0.0,
    entries: []}`` shell in ``__post_init__`` means that even a zero-issue
    cycle (or a crash before the first ``append_entry``) leaves the
    artefact on disk, which is what kill-condition tooling needs to
    distinguish "drained nothing" from "never ran".

    The filename embeds a UTC run-start timestamp with microsecond
    resolution, so two ``RunLog`` instances on the same ``cycle_id``
    (re-running ``drain-cycle`` after a halt) write to two separate
    files instead of one clobbering the other.
    """

    cycle_id: str
    path: Path = field(init=False)
    entries: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list, init=False)

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        directory = runs_dir()
        directory.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        run_started_at = datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)
        timestamp = run_started_at.strftime("%Y%m%dT%H%M%S%fZ")
        self.path = directory / f"{self.cycle_id}-{timestamp}.json"
        self._persist()

    def append_entry(
        self,
        *,
        issue_identifier: str,
        started_at: str,
        finished_at: str,
        exit_code: int,
        final_linear_state: str,
        worktree_path: str,
        halt_reason: str | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        self.entries.append(
            {
                "issue_identifier": issue_identifier,
                "started_at": started_at,
                "finished_at": finished_at,
                "exit_code": exit_code,
                "final_linear_state": final_linear_state,
                "worktree_path": worktree_path,
                "halt_reason": halt_reason,
            }
        )
        self._persist()

    def _persist(self) -> None:
        payload = {
            "cycle_id": self.cycle_id,
            "cycle_duration_seconds": self._cycle_duration_seconds(),
            "entries": self.entries,
        }
        self.path.write_text(json.dumps(payload, indent=2) + "\n")

    def _cycle_duration_seconds(self) -> float:
        if not self.entries:
            return 0.0
        starts = [datetime.fromisoformat(e["started_at"]) for e in self.entries]
        finishes = [datetime.fromisoformat(e["finished_at"]) for e in self.entries]
        return (max(finishes) - min(starts)).total_seconds()

content.text: I now have everything: full code structure, exact CLI capabilities, and quantified evidence. The diagnosis is clear. Before writing the plan, four decisions genuinely change what I build — let me settle them with you.

questions.question: The worker's token/cost data only exists if we capture claude's machine-readable output. How should we capture it?

options.label: Stream-json: live + logged
options.description: Orchestrator parses claude's stream-json events in real time → live token/cost/turn/elapsed in a `status` command AND final usage written to the run log. More code (Popen + stream parser), but satisfies both 'monitor while running' and 'log it'.

options.label: JSON result: logged only
options.description: Capture the single final JSON blob per issue (usage, cost, num_turns, duration) into the run log. Simpler. No live mid-issue token visibility — `status` shows only which issue + elapsed time.

questions.question: All 5 workers ran on Opus, which drove the ~108M-token burn (~$300+ equivalent). What model should drain-cycle workers use?

options.label: Sonnet by default
options.description: Workers default to claude-sonnet-4-6 (~5× cheaper than Opus). Configurable per run via a flag. Biggest single cost lever — Opus is what consumed the quota.

options.label: Keep Opus
options.description: Highest quality per issue, but this is exactly what burned the quota. Guardrails would be the only protection.

options.label: Required --model flag
options.description: No default; operator must choose the model each run. Forces a conscious cost decision but adds friction.

questions.question: What thresholds should kill a worker (per-issue cost $ + time, plus a cycle-wide cap)? These are dollar-equivalent API spend via --max-budget-usd.

options.label: Moderate
options.description: Per-issue: $10 or 20 min. Cycle-wide: $40 or 90 min. Would have killed ABA-205 (the 43M-token, 23-min issue) mid-flight. Sensible default for unattended runs.

options.label: Conservative
options.description: Per-issue: $5 or 12 min. Cycle-wide: $20 or 60 min. Tighter — better protection, higher chance of killing a legitimately long issue.

options.label: Generous
options.description: Per-issue: $25 or 40 min. Cycle-wide: $100 or 180 min. Looser — fewer false kills, weaker quota protection.

questions.question: Some plugins (e.g. entire.io) didn't load in the headless workers. How far should I take that now?

options.label: Observability-first, then reproduce
options.description: Ship usage + debug logging now, add a one-shot reproduction step to confirm exactly what does/doesn't load headless, and document the headless-vs-interactive divergence. Don't guess-fix the root cause blind.

options.label: Attempt a fix now
options.description: Try to force plugins/entire to load in the spawned session (explicit --plugin-dir/--settings or a SessionStart hook) as part of this change, accepting it may need iteration.

options.label: Defer entirely
options.description: Out of scope for this change; file a separate follow-up issue and focus only on observability + guardrails.

Answers captured. The model answer adds a per-ticket override (Sonnet default, ticket can specify), and the guardrail answer reframes the whole thing: you're on a **subscription**, so tokens — not dollars — are your mental model, and there's a big metric gap I need to surface. Let me reconcile it before writing the plan.

Your manual sessions run ~66.8k tokens (entire.io). But my measurement of the drain showed ABA-205 at **43M cache-read tokens**. These aren't the same metric: entire's "66.8k/session" is **peak context occupancy** (how full the window got on its biggest turn), while the quota-burning number is **cumulative tokens summed across every turn**. A manual issue is one continuous session ≈ one context fill. A drain worker took **49–234 turns**, and every turn re-reads the whole context — so cumulative balloons to millions even though peak-context stays ~150-200k. That's the gap, and the fix is to show **both** (peak context + cumulative + cost + turns), with the kill threshold on cumulative tokens since that's what drains the subscription.

Let me write the plan.

input.content:
# drain-cycle: diagnose quota burn + add token/time guardrails & live observability

## Context

The last unattended drain (`cycle 3fb7ba8a`, run `20260523T0738`) drained 5 issues in
77 minutes and consumed the operator's entire token quota. Manual work on the same kind
of issue costs ~10 min and a fraction of the tokens. The operator wants to (1) diagnose
what went wrong, (2) be able to monitor a live run (elapsed, progress, tokens, cost),
(3) have hard guardrails on time **and** tokens that kill the worker on breach, and
(4) have all of it written into the run log. A secondary observation — some normal
plugins (e.g. entire.io) didn't load in the headless workers — hints the spawn
environment diverges from an interactive session in ways that weren't intended.

## Diagnosis (evidence)

Token usage reconstructed from the worker session transcripts
(`~/.claude/projects/*worktrees*ABA-*/*.jsonl`), all on **`claude-opus-4-7`**:

| Issue   | Turns | Cache-read | Cache-create | Output | Wall-clock | Outcome |
|---------|------:|-----------:|-------------:|-------:|-----------:|---------|
| ABA-204 |   113 |     16.5M  |       0.99M  |  270K  |     18m    | Done    |
| ABA-205 |   234 |   **43.1M**|       1.23M  |  273K  |     23m    | Done    |
| ABA-134 |   192 |     34.1M  |       1.33M  |  341K  |     21m    | Done    |
| ABA-188 |    49 |      2.7M  |       0.50M  |   36K  |      3m    | Done    |
| ABA-133 |    98 |     11.3M  |       0.58M  |  141K  |     11m    | halt    |
| **Σ**   |  686  | **~108M**  |     ~4.6M    | ~1.06M |     77m    |         |

Root causes, in order of impact:

1. **Cumulative tokens, driven by turn count.** Quota burn = tokens summed across *every*
   turn. Each `claude -p` turn re-reads the whole accumulated context (cache-read), so an
   issue taking 234 turns reads ~180-200k tokens × 234 ≈ 43M. The healthy cheap issue
   (ABA-188) took 49 turns / 3.3M cumulative; the runaways took 100-234 turns.
2. **Opus on every worker.** `claude-opus-4-7` is pinned in user config and inherited by
   every spawn. Opus cache-read/output is ~5× Sonnet — the single biggest cost multiplier.
3. **Heavy per-issue workload.** The prompt (`prompt.py`) tells every worker to run the
   full `/code-review-and-quality` multi-agent skill + a fix loop + commit + push + Linear
   calls — far more turns than a manual edit.
4. **Zero observability.** The run log (`runlog.py`) records only timestamps and exit code
   — no tokens, cost, turns, or model. The operator was flying blind (cannot tell a
   working run from a runaway).
5. **No token/cost/turn guardrail.** Only a 3600s/issue wall-clock timeout exists; nothing
   caps tokens, cost, turns, or total cycle spend. `claude` *does* support
   `--max-budget-usd` (v2.1.150) — unused.
6. **Timeout kills only the direct child.** `subprocess.run(timeout=)` with no
   `start_new_session` leaves grandchildren (MCP servers, sub-agents) orphaned and still
   consuming on a timeout.

**Metric reconciliation (important for threshold calibration).** entire.io reports the
operator's manual sessions at ~66.8k tokens avg (~30k Sonnet, ~100k Opus). That figure is
**peak context occupancy** (largest single turn), not cumulative. A manual issue ≈ one
context fill; an autonomous worker = N turns × context. So the guardrail and display must
show **both**: peak-context (comparable to entire's number) *and* cumulative tokens (the
quota-relevant number), alongside cost and turn count.

## Decisions (from operator)

- **Monitoring:** stream-json — parse `claude`'s event stream live; show progress and log
  final usage.
- **Worker model:** default `claude-sonnet-4-6`; a `model:` Linear label on the issue
  overrides (mirrors the existing `repo:` label mechanism). Ticket wins when set.
- **Guardrails:** kill on time **and** tokens; show cost + tokens together in context.
  Subscription user → token count is primary, cost shown alongside.
- **Plugin issue:** observability-first — capture what loads, add a reproduction step,
  document the divergence; do **not** blind-fix the root cause this round.

## Approach

### 1. Verification spike (gate — do first)
Run once, read-only, to ground the parser in real output (no assuming the schema):
```
claude -p --verbose --output-format stream-json --max-budget-usd 0.50 "say hi"
```
Capture the exact event shapes: the `system`/`init` event, per-turn `assistant` events
(`message.usage`: `input_tokens`, `output_tokens`, `cache_creation_input_tokens`,
`cache_read_input_tokens`), and the terminal `result` event (`total_cost_usd`, `usage`,
`num_turns`, `duration_ms`, `session_id`, `is_error`, `subtype`). Confirm `--verbose` is
required with `stream-json` in `-p`. **Gate:** only build the parser against confirmed
fields; flag any field that isn't present.

### 2. Worker model resolution — `model:` label
New small resolver mirroring `repos.py`'s `repo:`-label extraction: read a `model:<alias>`
label off `issue["labels"]`, default `claude-sonnet-4-6`. Pass `--model <resolved>` into
the worker command. Unknown/missing label → default. Keep it a pure function for testing.

### 3. Spawn refactor — `drain_cycle/worker.py` (new)
Extract the spawn block (orchestrator.py:143-178) into a `worker.run_issue(...)` that owns
the streamed, guarded spawn and returns a result struct
(`exit_code`, `usage`, `cost_usd`, `num_turns`, `peak_context_tokens`, `session_id`,
`is_error`, `kill_reason`). Implementation:
- `subprocess.Popen([claude, -p, --verbose, --output-format, stream-json,
  --model, <m>, --max-budget-usd, <cap>, --dangerously-skip-permissions, <prompt>],
  cwd=worktree, stdout=PIPE, stderr=STDOUT, text=True, bufsize=1, start_new_session=True)`.
  `start_new_session=True` makes the child a process-group leader so we can reap
  grandchildren.
- Read `p.stdout` line by line; parse each JSON event. On each event: accumulate cumulative
  usage + cost + turns, track peak context, update the live marker (step 5), emit a compact
  progress line to stderr, and check guardrails (step 4).
- A `threading.Timer` (per-issue time cap) + cycle-deadline check kills the **process
  group** (`os.killpg(os.getpgid(p.pid), SIGTERM)` then `SIGKILL`) so a quiet long turn
  still trips the time guard. Cancel the timer on normal completion.
- Capture the terminal `result` event for the run-log entry.
- `--max-budget-usd` is the native belt; the orchestrator-side cumulative-token + time caps
  are the suspenders.

### 4. Guardrails — `drain_cycle/limits.py` (new)
Thresholds with baked-in defaults, overridable by an optional `~/.drain-cycle/limits.yml`
and/or CLI flags. Defaults (evidence-grounded, tunable — first real run will calibrate):
- Per-issue: **8M cumulative tokens** · **20 min** · **$15** (`--max-budget-usd`).
- Cycle-wide: **30M cumulative tokens** · **90 min** · **$60**.
On breach: kill the worker's process group, record a halt entry whose `halt_reason` names
the breached limit and the value at kill time, and stop the cycle (same exit-1 + revert
contract as existing halts). Each guardrail is independently on/off-able.

### 5. Live observability — `drain_cycle/progress.py` + `status.py` (new)
- `progress.py`: write/read/clear `~/.drain-cycle/active.json` (one level **above**
  `runs/` so `grade.py`'s `runs/*.json` glob never sees it). Marker carries:
  `pid`, `cycle_id`, `run_log_path`, `issue{identifier,title,repo,worktree_path}`,
  `model`, `started_at`, `index`, `total`, and a live `progress` block
  (`turns`, `cumulative_tokens`, `peak_context_tokens`, `cost_usd`, `elapsed_seconds`,
  `last_event_at`). Updated on every stream event; cleared in a `finally` covering all exit
  paths. `pid` lets `status` detect a stale marker (dead process).
- `status.py` (`drain-cycle status`): read the marker and print a human view —
  which issue, model, `[index/total]`, elapsed, turns, **tokens (cumulative + peak) and
  cost shown together**, against the configured caps (e.g. `12.4M / 8M ⚠`). Live marker →
  live view; dead pid → stale view; no marker → point at `grade`/run logs.

  `ABA-205 · turn 42 · 8.1M tok (peak 180k) · $12.30 · 14m`.

`grade.py` reads only `cycle_id` + `entries[].final_linear_state`, so new keys are
backward-compatible. Add to each entry: `model`, `usage` (input/output/cache_create/
cache_read/cumulative + peak_context), `cost_usd`, `num_turns`, `session_id`, `is_error`,
explicit `duration_seconds`. Add top-level aggregates: `cycle_cost_usd`,
`cycle_tokens_cumulative`. Halt entries from a guardrail breach carry the breach detail in
`halt_reason`. Update the module docstring schema block.

### 7. Plugin / entire.io investigation (observability-first)

  `entire` is a standalone binary (`~/.local/bin/entire`), not in user `enabledPlugins` and
  not a user hook — so headless `-p` never triggered its checkpointing. Plugins enabled at
  user level *should* load under `-p` (only `--bare` skips plugin sync), so the divergence
  needs runtime confirmation, not a guess.

  log) so the next run records exactly which plugins/MCP/hooks initialised.
- Add a documented one-shot reproduction step (run `claude -p` headless in a worktree, diff
  loaded plugins vs interactive) to `docs/design-decisions.md`, and a decision entry naming
  the headless-vs-interactive divergence as a known constraint with the open question. No
  code fix to plugin loading this round.

### 8. Docs
- `README.md`: document `drain-cycle status`, the `model:` label, the guardrail limits +
  `limits.yml`, and the new run-log fields.
- `docs/design-decisions.md`: entries for stream-json+Popen+process-group kill, the
  token/cost/time guardrails and defaults, default-Sonnet + `model:` override, the
  active-marker location (above `runs/`), and the plugin-divergence open question.

## Files

- **New:** `drain_cycle/worker.py`, `drain_cycle/progress.py`, `drain_cycle/status.py`,
  `drain_cycle/limits.py`, plus a `model:`-label resolver (small fn, in `repos.py` next to
  the `repo:` logic or its own `model.py`).
- **Edit:** `drain_cycle/orchestrator.py` (call `worker.run_issue`, index/total enumerate,
  marker write/clear, cycle-deadline + cycle-token aggregate, guardrail-breach halt path),
  `drain_cycle/runlog.py` (extra fields + aggregates + docstring), `drain_cycle/cli.py`
  (`status` dispatch + usage), `drain_cycle/prompt.py` (only if model/guardrail context
  needs surfacing to the worker), `README.md`, `docs/design-decisions.md`.
- **Tests:** `tests/test_worker.py` (stream parse, guardrail kill via fake claude script
  emitting stream-json, process-group kill), `tests/test_progress.py`,
  `tests/test_status.py`, `tests/test_limits.py`, `model:`-label resolution test, run-log

  dispatch test. Reuse existing test conventions (`_issue(...)` helper, fake-claude shell
  script via `_CLAUDE_CMD` monkeypatch, `monkeypatch.setenv("HOME", tmp_path)`).

## Verification

1. **Spike gate:** event schema confirmed against real `claude -p --output-format
   stream-json` output before the parser is written.
2. **Unit suite green:** `uv run pytest` — including a fake-claude script that emits a
   scripted stream-json sequence and one that overshoots a tiny token cap to prove the
   process-group kill fires and records the right `halt_reason`.

   deliberately tiny per-issue token cap; confirm (a) `drain-cycle status` shows live
   tokens+cost+turns+elapsed from another terminal, (b) the worker is killed on cap breach
   and its whole process group dies (no orphaned `claude`/MCP procs — check `pgrep -fl
   claude`), (c) the run-log entry carries model/usage/cost/turns/session_id and the breach
   reason, (d) the `--debug-file` capture shows which plugins loaded headless.

   like ABA-205 while letting a healthy issue like ABA-188 finish; adjust `limits.yml`.

## Linear

ABA-234 "status command + streaming", which this **absorbs** — fold its scope in and close
